programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Seals: Tanja Rosenberg is part of a seal rescue team in Friedrichskoog in the far north of Germany. Many seals lose their mothers for various reasons each year – Tanja and her crew make sure they survive, grow strong and can be returned to the North Sea again. Birds of Prey: Karl Fischer is the manager of the sanctuary for birds of prey. One of his specialties is the upbringing of bald eagles – like his newest fosterling Ingo. But Karl does not want Ingo to grow up alone and so he introduces him to a young eagle owl named Klaus. Though the two make sure that Karl knows how much they dislike each other and keep bickering and biting one another, they are both suspiciously sad, when Klaus is moving to a different zoo… Platypus: Kangaroo-nanny Margit Cianelli is nervous: A tiny motherless platypus has been found by hikers and been brought to her house. She has never cared for a platypus and is not sure if everything is alright with the little guy. A visit at the vet’s is to bring clarity.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Kangaroo Kira: Karin Traub has dedicated her life to saving injured and orphaned kangaroo babies in Australia. Currently she shares her trailer with a whole kangaroo kindergarten! Her newest problem child is the tiny kangaroo Kira. Kira does not want to drink her milk. Luckily Karin has a big bag of tricks and knows just what to do. Racoon Dog Mitja: Nicola Gölzner is raising an unusual little animal at her house. But racoon dog Mitja is so cute, that even her dog has taken a liking to the little guy. Today the three of them go to check out a wildlife park near Nicola’s house, where Mitja is to live as soon as he is big enough. How will Mitja like his future home? Warthogs: On the “Daktari Farm” in Namibia two warthog babies named Lilly and Piggy are utterly besotted with their nanny Bärbel Hahn. They have completely accepted her as a surrogate mom – but just like real children, they do not always behave the way she wants them to. As soon as she turns her back, the little rascals are up to new pranks. Lucky for them that Bärbel’s patience is sheer inexhaustible.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An orphaned baby baboon has enjoyed the love of its human foster mother at CARE, an emergency wildlife center. How will he respond when he meets his first baboon friend? This is an important milestone in becoming a member of a troop. Animal rescuers from the Wildlife Foundation in Namibia need to catch a young female cheetah in captivity; this is tricky and potentially dangerous work. Their plan is to reunite her with her mother so that she can learn how to function and survive in the wild. Bush babies are especially cute so sometimes they are kept as pets. The care givers at Riverside Wildlife Centre put emotions aside and prepare one for release in the wild.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Rhino horn is more valuable than gold and more recent increases in poaching leaves the survival of this species at risk. Every day a rhino monitoring team at Somkhanda Game Reserve keeps track of a rhino and her calf, but skilful tracking and telemetry don’t guarantee that they’ll find them. When it comes to their milk feed, the competition is fierce for four orphaned baby dassies at Daktari Wildlife Orphanage. Their care taker forms strong bonds with the animals he looks after and decides to visit the rocky outcrop where he’s released a dassie – can he find his friend? A baby porcupine is hand-reared at Tenikwa Wildlife and Rehabilitation Centre. Can his surrogate mom teach him to forage; otherwise they won’t be able to release him back in the wild.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Raising wildlife can be a long-term commitment: elephant calves are dependent on their mothers for three years and chimpanzees rely on special attention for about seven. In this episode, we visit the Lilayi Elephant Nursery in Zambia and the Mefou Primate Park in Cameroon to meet the highly committed people who take on this responsibility. Night or day, a baby elephant can never be left alone and many of the chimpanzee orphans arrive highly traumatised. Their care givers need to work very hard to restore their trust and will to live. At the Kwaggasvlaktes Wildlife Centre in South Africa some baby Wildebeest rely on heart-warming help from humans and an unusual sheep to survive.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An African penguin chick has survived expulsion from the nest, but can surgery help an adult who’s been savaged by a seal to see the ocean again? All in a day’s job at the South African Marine Rehabilitation and Education Centre where a caregiver’s fingers also narrowly escape the razor-sharp beak of a gannet. Something stands in the way of a high spirited young caracal, but the Sondela Wildlife Centre are hatching a plan to secure his release back in the wild. Two baby warthogs at the Gitta Martula Rehabilitation Wildlife Centre still prefer their apples to their potatoes but, slowly and surely, they are being adjusted to living in their natural environment – the whole wild world awaits.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Rehabilitating and teaching young wild dog packs to hunt is what Margerita does at Erindi Private Game Reserve in Namibia. Margerita had discovered many of the wild dog packs in the region had succumb to illness and died. Margerita took it upon herself to raise an abandoned young pack of wild dogs. She teaches them to run as a pack, hunt as a pack and survive as a pack thus ensuring when the time comes the wild dog pack will survive in the wild. Alma’s passion for yellow baboons in Malawi brought her to the African continent to live-out her passion. Her daily chores, activities and inspections keep her busy. Two yellow baboons meet each other for the first time when the gate separating them is opened. One of South Africa’s smallest antelope known as a Cape Grys Bokkie loves playing hide ‘n seek it seems. When it comes to feeding the young antelope it always proves to be a challenge. However, this could be the nature of this timid little antelope.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Lindi, the one-eyed Lion cub lives at Wildthingz Lodge situated in the northern region of South Africa. Having one eye does not restrict Lindi from been a playful and energetic lion cub. Skabenga, a young Hyena, also lives at Wildthingz. Skabenga has almost become part of the family, and his bond to his caretaker cannot be denied. Wolves in Africa? Tsitsikamma Wolf Sanctuary is an orphanage to abandoned wolves. Raising wolves on the other hand is not always an easy job. This is the case with an energetic seven-month-old wolf, Raul who knows no boundaries. We meet Victor Hugo at Somkhanda where the motto is to keep all the animals wild while rehabilitating them. We will meet a herd of Zebra and their young and discover that the dynamics in the family group are not always what they seem. Victor also rescues a family of owl hatchlings and ensures their safety by building a new house in a nearby tree.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: It’s not surprising that raising the fastest animal on the planet involves regular exercise; Frank Kasakamula at the Ranch Conservancy in South Africa has a number of strategies in place for the cheetah in his care. A traumatised, orphaned baby Vervet monkey is especially clingy and demanding. But from nappy rash to night feeds, Dean Oakes at the Bambelela Wildlife Sanctuary takes everything involved in being a ‘good mother’ in his stride. Everyone at the Harnas Wildlife Foundation in Namibia has a right to life, including a blind, baby kudu named Derek. Soon his care taker, Louis Duvenhage, will introduce him to its new friends: a Springbok and a Duiker.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: In the Centre for Rehabilitation of Wildlife situated in KwaZulu Natal, South Africa, junior nurse Kylie is preparing grey duiker Major for his release into the wild. Major had suffered several injuries when he arrived at the centre, but with Kylie’s help he recovered, and he will soon be back in his natural environment. We also travel to the Eastern Cape in South Africa to the main port city called, Port Elizabeth. Here Ruth, a dedicated animal caretaker, is constantly monitoring her loggerhead turtle hatchling Rocky, who has trouble with weight fluctuations. But once his nutrition is balanced enough that he can keep his weight for several weeks, he can be released into the Indian Ocean.Deep in the Cameroonian jungles we meet Rachel, a committed caretaker of primates at the Mefoue Primate Sanctuary. She arrived at the centre sixteen years ago and decided to stay after Khan Daniel, a new-born silverback gorilla, chose her as his surrogate mother. Since then her passion for these animals grew stronger from day to day. Sixteen years later Khan Daniel is the leader of the gorilla troop while Rachel now runs the private facility caring for these gentle giants of the forest.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Howick in Kwa-Zulu Natal, South Africa to a Centre called FreeMe Wildlife Rehabilitation. Here we meet Kirsten, also known as ‘Otter Mom number 1’. Kirsten is training her three orphaned Otters how to swim and hunt. The three mammals, Juliet, Oberon and Othelloe have almost completed their rehabilitation. After preparing the release site for two weeks it is time to return the three siblings back into the river. But persuading them to leave their temporary home might be more of a challenge than Kirsten anticipated. Situated at the base of the Hlumu Hlumu Mountains in the province of Mpumalanga in South Africa we find DIY Wildlife Rehabilitation. Animal caretaker Mitch oversees many different animals at his sanctuary. He is preparing to transport and release a troop of Mongooses onto a new farm. Mongooses play a vital role in the region by keeping the snake population in balance. And in the summer season there are lots of them. So mobilizing the family quickly and efficiently is the first priority. A one-hour drive east of Johannesburg, at Wildlife, Judy is rehabilitating a one month old Squirrel. This tiny rodent has been kept as a pet and was malnourished. Judy constantly has to monitor his weight. But the little animal seems to become stronger and more confident because is obviously doesn’t want to be treated like a baby anymore. So Judy’s efforts pay off in the end.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.sub-title: White Rhino, Giant Pouched Rat, Bat
programme.desc: At the Hoedspruit Endangered Species Centre in the province of Limpopo we meet a member of one of the most threatened species on the planet, a white rhino named Esme. Caretaker Natasha is looking after the nine-month-old and makes sure it can develop in a well-protected surrounding. And she has got some help from a rather unexpected companion for the little calf - a dog named David. Further north we visit Tanzania, where so-called “Hero Rats” are fulfilling special tasks. These giant pouched rats are able to detect landmines and sniff out diseases. One of these little heroes named Sykwalker is learning how to detect tuberculosis. Caretaker Estadiov provides us an insight into the training of these extraordinary animals. In our highlight story, we meet Jane and her orphaned bat Devan. Rewild in Limpopo is the only Centre in the area that cares for and rehabilitates bats. Devan is having trouble with an essential skill for every bat – flying or more specifically the take-off. Because once he is airborne he seems to master the art of flight effortlessly.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An hour’s drive from Johannesburg Warren is raising three cheetah cubs at the Lion and Cheetah Sanctuary. They are an endangered species – not only in South Africa but around the world. Warren is taking the three orphaned siblings for walks on a daily basis to give them the opportunity to experience being outside their enclosure. This is part of the preparation for their return to the wild. Convincing them to leave the enclosure is easy, trying to get them back in is a difficult task to undertake. In the South African Limpopo province, we meet clinic manager Nikita at the Moholoholo Animal Rehabilitation Centre. She’s taking care of several different animals all of whom got their own sad story. One of them is a female honey badger who recently lost her cub post-partum. She is mothering a chain as a substitute for her cub so Nikita is trying to help her getting through this difficult phase. She is also monitoring vultures who have fallen victim to poisoning. And she introduces Havana to us - a juvenile caracal who was found abandoned in a hotel. She has obviously been raised by humans as she was found clinging to a milk bottle. In our highlight story we travel to Crag’s View Wild Care Centre in Port Edwards along the south coast of Kwa-Zulu Natal. Here we meet Craig and a partially blind blue duiker. Craig didn’t have high hopes for the antelope to survive, as she has been attacked by a large cat. But thanks to the passionate caretaker the bubbly animal is doing great today.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.sub-title: Wild Dog, Owls, Bat Eared Foxes
programme.desc: At the Loebis Predator Park in Bela-Bella, a town in the South African Limpopo region, caretaker Annel is looking after orphaned animal babys. Part of her gang of little rascals are a wild dog puppy and a porcupine – both two months old – who have an unlikely friendship and team up against a greedy little predator who likes to steel their food – a same-aged lion cub. But in the end, all of them get along very well – even with the cheeky little mongooses. The highlight of our visit there is the birth of two new members of this unusual family. We also meet Deidre Joubert at the Wild and Free Wildlife Rehabilitation Centre in Mpumalanga who takes care of young barn owls and prepares a bunch of them for their return to the wild. The first step of this process is a release into a bigger enclosure, in which they will stay for seven days, before they finally get back to their natural environment. In our highlight story, we meet sister and brother team Chriszanne and Nicol Burger at the Felidae Wildlife Centre just outside a town called Kimberley in the South African province of Northern Cape. Their focus is on rescued wild cats of all sizes but they also take care for a family of bat-eared foxes – a very intelligent and brash species.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: In the Wokutakula Wildlife Rescue Centre located in Brits, 85 kilometres north of Johannesburg animal caretaker Juan is looking after a partially blind juvenile Tiger called Felix. Juan rescued the exotic animal just moments before it was going to be traded overseas. He tried to treat the tiger’s eye condition but so far, his efforts were unsuccessful. There is no hope for Felix to be returned to the wild, but she has adapted to her new life – thanks to other Tigers, who accompany her and Juan, who sheperds her as good as he can. In the province of Limpopo, we meet Laita at the Moholoholo Animal Rehabilitation Centre. She takes care of two precious juveniles: a Black Leopard and a Serval. The Black Leopard was raised by humans and didn’t get the right nutrition, so he was stunted in growth. Therefore, it wouldn’t have any chance to survive in the wild. Laita oversees his rehabilitation and the young animal makes great strides. The Serval is the only survivor of four abandoned kittens that got rescued. He also comes along very well and is developing essential skills. For our highlight story we travel to the Mankwe Wildlife Reserve, where Dougal and Lynn are monitoring young and juvenile animals. Spotting giraffes and rhinos isn’t easy but it’s a rewarding task.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Baboon Mother: In South Africa, 76-yr. old Rita Miljo is raising the tiny baboon baby Rosie as if it were her own. As Rosie grows bigger and stronger, Rita takes her to a “baboon kindergarten”, where she is to form a new troop with others. We also witness Rita and her team releasing a troop of grown baboons into the wild. Wombat Baby Mater: Shaun Douglas of “Something Wild Wildlife Sanctuary” in Tasmania has become a surrogate “mom” to the tiny wombat Mater. As little wombats cling to their mothers for many months in their early lives, Shaun takes Mater with him wherever he goes – even to the shower and to bed!

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Orcas: The 2-yr. old orca girl Skyla it to become part of the orca show at the “Loro Parque” in Tenerife. Her nannies Mark Galán and Claudia Vollhardt think of many different games and costumes they can use to teach Skyla the different tricks. Will Skyla finally master the difficult “centerbelly”? Bat-Hospital: Xanadu, a one week old flying fox baby, enjoys the loving care of veterinarian Jeanny MacLean and animal carer Sandra Kühne. Also two new babies arrive at the busy Australian hospital: Paulie and Sophie have to say goodbye to their prior nanny Fay. Will they adapt to the hundreds of bats that live at the Jeanny’s hospital?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: White Tiger Pipo: Nanny Regina Hamza has been raising a white tiger cub named Pipo. As Pipo is now growing too strong and wild for Regina and her husband, he is to return to the Safari Zoo “Serengeti Park” and live with a white tiger dame named Bianca. But saying goodbye is not easy for Regina, and Pipo is not so sure how he will like his new home with Bianca… Seals: Francois Hugo is fighting for the lives of the seal population of South Africa – his unusual approach and love for these creatures has created a very close bond. He has adopted infant seals JT, Alpha and Amigo in his first attempt to raise three pups at once.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Elk Baby: Two week old elk baby Flori was left behind by his mother and now lives with four dogs and his foster father Harald - in his living room! Will he be able to reunite with his parents? Koala Robiana: 9 month old Koala Robiana was left without a mother and with many burns after a forest fire. Now nanny Lorraine takes loving care of the foundling. With the help koala expert Rolf Schlagloth, she hopes to get Robiana fit enough to return her to wild soon. Lion Babies: Dr. Michael Böer is the veterinarian at the “Serengeti Park” near Hannover. We join him on one of his check-up tours through the park to see how the animals and especially their babies are doing. Four new lion cubs have recently been born – a highlight even for the experienced wildlife doctor!

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Rhino Baby Layla: Rhinoceros baby Layla is a small sensation – she is the first artificially inseminated rhino baby ever. Dr. Victor Molnár lovingly looks after his precious baby patient day and night – but will he be able to reunite her with her parents? Mountain Rescue Dog: In the Tirolian Mountains, 6 month old puppy Janis starts her training to become a mountain rescue dog. Her trainer Alois Haselwanter is putting her through the paces for working in the wilderness. Will she meet the challenges of dog training school and become a member of the Tirolian mountain rescue dog team? Genet Leonard: In South Africa, genet foundling Leonard makes his first excursion into the wild. Will he make new friends outside the sheltered home of his nanny Alison Horn?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Wildlife-Hospital Healesville: A thrilling day at the Australian Wildlife Health Centre for veterinarian Kelly O’Sulivan and her assistant Guni Hudson: An Australian Hedgehog was hit by a car and is badly injured. Kelly fights for the life of an Opossum. And Anya, a baby wallaby, makes her first exciting public appearance at the centre. Elephant Malaika: We spend the day with handler Joseph and the 4-yr. old Elephant Malaika. Jospeh has known Malaika since she was a few weeks old. How will she do with the children on her first visit to a public school?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Dolphin Joan: Dolphin Joan is the darling of visitors to the “Loro Parque” and will face the challenge of his first live show in front of an audience. His trainer Miguel Luis Sosa is going through the final training lessons with him. Will this quick learner be able to perform with the help of his mother and grandmother? Vervet Monkey Alfie: Silke von Eynern is runs the “Bambelela Farm” in South Africa, where she has taken care of vervet monkeys for many years. Her newest arrival is Alfie, a vervet monkey that has been kept as a pet and been treated all wrong. Silke is on a mission to teach 10 weeks old Alfie how to be a proper monkey. Wildlife Foundlings: It’s high season at the wild animal station Sachsenhagen. Mareen Möller takes care of all sorts of foundlings and injured orphans -- she has her hands full all day. Especially a baby fox named Didi and a wild boar baby named Armin require all her attention as they are growing tall and bold.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Tree-Kangaroo Dorothy: Margit Cianelli is a trained animal carer who lives at the Lumholtz Lodge in the Australian tablelands and is currently taking care of small tree kangaroo named Dorothy. Dorothy is old and strong enough to go into the forest by herself now. Margit gets nervous, when Dorothy does not return before dusk. Luckily she is wearing a tracking device… Warthog Baby: Nelly is a 5 week old Warthog that lives in the Kragga Kamma Game Park in South Africa. Her nanny Heike Zitzer is trying to wean Nelly from her care, but the little warthog just loves being by her side. When Heike leaves her alone, Nelly meets grumpy fellows as she searches for Heike throughout the park. Will Nelly make new friends in the end?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Arctic Wolves: A pack of 5 week old wolf cubs get ready to go to school with famous film animal trainer Joe Bodemann. But before that, the little cubs have to be weaned off their bottles and get used to their new outdoor enclosure. Wildlife Warriors: These special nannies are always ready to jump into action to save animals of every kind. Over 100 patients are being treated by this Australian wildlife hospital every day: tortoises, kookaburras, wild boars, possums, hedgehogs - and even poisonous snakes! Lion Amir: In South Africa, 11 month old lion Amir was brought to his nanny Katja Peters half starved. Now he’s maturing into a beautiful lion and is looking for company. Will he find true love with a very special lioness?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Raccoon Zorro: 8 week old racoon Zorro is a real handful for Nanny Julia Wiertz. No stone is left unturned on his mischievous adventures. Will Zorro be able to learn how to climb and scavage for food with Julia as a teacher? Storks: Dr. Michael Kaatz has two motherless baby storks at his stork sanctuary. In order to find a new home for Max and Moritz, he is trying to trick a stork family into adopting the orphaned storks. But will the recovered foundlings ever be able to fly in the wild? Zebra Baby et al.: Wildlife keeper Jaco Nell teaches his new assistant Anna de la Roca how to take care of cheetahs, rhinos and zebras on his South African gaming reserve “Kapama”.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Emus: The two emu babies Candy and Henry have accidentally imprinted on their care taker Matthias Reinschmidt at the “Loro Parque” in Tenerife. Now the two young birds follow Matthias everywhere he goes. But they are now big enough to live in an outdoor enclosure of the park – alongside giant turtles! Will they accept their new home and the fact that Matthias will only come to visit sporadically from now on? Baboon Babies: In Namibia, Corinna Auffing and her husband John Ross Stewart are teaching the three little baboon babies Drekki, Stocki and Beuki everything they need to know in order to become proper baboons. Elephant Limpopo: Elephant Baby Limpopo joins the herd on a South African elephant safari under the guidance of Nanny Paul Coetzee for the first time.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Pilot Nanny: At the South African “Sondéla Wildlife Center” nanny Petronel Nieuwoudt takes care of animals that have been injured or orphaned because of poaching. Her newest “problem child” is a gnu named Cabrona – will she find a new family for the shy animal? Penguins: Nanny Wolfgang Magnus is responsible for the penguin offspring in Germany’s famous bird park in Walsrode. He spends all the time he can with them, makes sure they gain enough weight and even builds them little caves made out of paper-mâché – several times daily!

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Cheetahs & Co.: Dana Vogt has a heart for all African animals, but it is especially the cheetahs that she has fallen in love with. She is returning to the “Harnas Farm” for the third year in a row and is amazed by how much the little babies she raised in her first year have grown. Monkey Annie: Annie is a tiny squirrel monkey that lives in the South African monkey sanctuary “Monkeyland”. Lara Mostert has been raising her so far, but now it is time to say goodbye, as Annie is to go live with other monkeys. But will they get along? Mini Pigs: Tina John has an unusual passion: mini pigs! Two of her cute pig pets are to move to a new farm. Tina is nervous: Will her little friends find a good new home?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Kangaroos: Gabi Friebe is an experienced kangaroo nanny at the Long Grass Nature Refuge in Australia. Gabi is worried: Some of her fosterlings are not doing well at all, and the little kangaroo Skippy is spending his first day alone outside. But why is he taking so long to come home at night? Mice, Duikers & Co.: Students Laura and Susanne are spending an unusual vacation on the “Daktari Farm” in South Africa. Besides spending their days nursing mice babies and African bush squirrels, they take care of a blind antelope baby named Emily.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Wombat Brady: Manfred and Helen Heide run a wombat sanctuary they call the “Wombat Hilton”. Brady is their latest “guest”. Together with wombat girl Chloé, Brady spends his days being cuddled by the Heides and hanging around in a fabric pouch. Soon he gets bored, however, and is happy to go an excursion... Wolves: Pascale Jüch has an unusual job: As a surrogate mom to three little wolves, she spends her day cuddling and nursing the wolf babies. In order for her to teach them proper pack behaviour, it is important that they accept her as the alpha animal. Pascale hopes that soon she will be able to integrate her fosterlings into another wolf pack. Kudu: Kudu Baby Kuduku was raised on the farm “Friedensort” in Namibia. The farmer’s son Marco Syvertsen is Kuduku’s best friend. But now it is time for the little Kudu to return to the wild. Marco and Kuduku take their first walk together outside the farm. Will Kuduku eventually appreciate the intimidating wilderness?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Pademelons: Pademelon baby Taila was raised by her nanny Julia Butler-Ross in her house. She is now growing too big and is to resettle in a new space outside. Obviously she is not to grow lonely there, and so Julia has a big surprise in store for Taila. Zebra: On the “Solitaire Guest Farm” in Namibia lives a young Zebra named Stripies who has just about reached puberty. His nanny Simone Swarts is amazed how similar his state is to human puberty: The zebra is moody and keeps pushing its borders. All the other animals are rather annoyed with Stripies, and Simone is worried that he will not be able to make friends with them again. But suddenly there is a small calf that does not budge from his sight. Has Stripies found a friend after all? Flying Foxes: Animal carer Roy Bäthe has found two motherless flying foxes at the zoo in Erfurt. He is now raising little Yoda and Ewok at home. They are developing great. There is just one question to be answered: How is Roy going to teach them how to fly?

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Penguins: Injured and orphaned penguins have found a new temporary home at the “Penguin Rehab Centre” in South Africa. Nanny Fran Grobbelaars newest foundling is a small penguin named Madala. In her care, Madala has recovered from the extreme injuries he had suffered – but now he has to learn many things anew. Fran teaches him everything he needs to know, from catching fish to swimming. Lion Baby Alvin: The small lion named Alvin was rescued from a Bavarian farm where he was not kept according to his kind. He was sick and shy. Through the efforts of the “Wildlife Nannies” crew, he found a new home at Joe Bodemann’s park for film animals. It takes Joe some time to win the Alvin’s trust – but eventually Alvin grows to be a healthy and strong lion. The training for his film career can start! Springbok: The Namibian “Omaruru Gamelodge” is a small paradise for more than 2.000 animals. Nanny Thomas Domenig is an expert of raising them all. We join him on the daily excitement of raising wild animals – his newest nurse child is a small springbok.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Gut Aiderbichl: Many stories take place at the animal sanctuary Gut Aiderbichl. Nanny Rico Fernando raises five orphaned fox babies as well as a tiny goatling named Franziska. Martina Neureiter makes sure an injured fawn named Bambi learns to walk again. Sharks: When they are fully grown, they look wild and scary – but even sharks do not enter this world as adults. Nanny Birger Kreutz knows how tiny and helpless predatory fish can be right after they are born. In this episode, he tries to get his newborn dogfish to join some stingray children in a new aquarium – not an easy task! Bat-eared Foxes: Margit Hülpert is the founder of the “bat-eared fox project” in Namibia that is dedicated to scientific research and preservation of the unusual animals. For the past months, she has been raising bat-eared fox named Monty. Now the time has come to release him to the wild. They spend a last fun, but emotional day together.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Seals: Tanja Rosenberg is part of a seal rescue team in Friedrichskoog in the far north of Germany. Many seals lose their mothers for various reasons each year – Tanja and her crew make sure they survive, grow strong and can be returned to the North Sea again. Birds of Prey: Karl Fischer is the manager of the sanctuary for birds of prey. One of his specialties is the upbringing of bald eagles – like his newest fosterling Ingo. But Karl does not want Ingo to grow up alone and so he introduces him to a young eagle owl named Klaus. Though the two make sure that Karl knows how much they dislike each other and keep bickering and biting one another, they are both suspiciously sad, when Klaus is moving to a different zoo… Platypus: Kangaroo-nanny Margit Cianelli is nervous: A tiny motherless platypus has been found by hikers and been brought to her house. She has never cared for a platypus and is not sure if everything is alright with the little guy. A visit at the vet’s is to bring clarity.

programme.title: Wildlife Nannies
programme.desc: Kangaroo Kira: Karin Traub has dedicated her life to saving injured and orphaned kangaroo babies in Australia. Currently she shares her trailer with a whole kangaroo kindergarten! Her newest problem child is the tiny kangaroo Kira. Kira does not want to drink her milk. Luckily Karin has a big bag of tricks and knows just what to do. Racoon Dog Mitja: Nicola Gölzner is raising an unusual little animal at her house. But racoon dog Mitja is so cute, that even her dog has taken a liking to the little guy. Today the three of them go to check out a wildlife park near Nicola’s house, where Mitja is to live as soon as he is big enough. How will Mitja like his future home? Warthogs: On the “Daktari Farm” in Namibia two warthog babies named Lilly and Piggy are utterly besotted with their nanny Bärbel Hahn. They have completely accepted her as a surrogate mom – but just like real children, they do not always behave the way she wants them to. As soon as she turns her back, the little rascals are up to new pranks. Lucky for them that Bärbel’s patience is sheer inexhaustible.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An orphaned baby baboon has enjoyed the love of its human foster mother at CARE, an emergency wildlife center. How will he respond when he meets his first baboon friend? This is an important milestone in becoming a member of a troop. Animal rescuers from the Wildlife Foundation in Namibia need to catch a young female cheetah in captivity; this is tricky and potentially dangerous work. Their plan is to reunite her with her mother so that she can learn how to function and survive in the wild. Bush babies are especially cute so sometimes they are kept as pets. The care givers at Riverside Wildlife Centre put emotions aside and prepare one for release in the wild.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Rhino horn is more valuable than gold and more recent increases in poaching leaves the survival of this species at risk. Every day a rhino monitoring team at Somkhanda Game Reserve keeps track of a rhino and her calf, but skilful tracking and telemetry don’t guarantee that they’ll find them. When it comes to their milk feed, the competition is fierce for four orphaned baby dassies at Daktari Wildlife Orphanage. Their care taker forms strong bonds with the animals he looks after and decides to visit the rocky outcrop where he’s released a dassie – can he find his friend? A baby porcupine is hand-reared at Tenikwa Wildlife and Rehabilitation Centre. Can his surrogate mom teach him to forage; otherwise they won’t be able to release him back in the wild.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Raising wildlife can be a long-term commitment: elephant calves are dependent on their mothers for three years and chimpanzees rely on special attention for about seven. In this episode, we visit the Lilayi Elephant Nursery in Zambia and the Mefou Primate Park in Cameroon to meet the highly committed people who take on this responsibility. Night or day, a baby elephant can never be left alone and many of the chimpanzee orphans arrive highly traumatised. Their care givers need to work very hard to restore their trust and will to live. At the Kwaggasvlaktes Wildlife Centre in South Africa some baby Wildebeest rely on heart-warming help from humans and an unusual sheep to survive.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An African penguin chick has survived expulsion from the nest, but can surgery help an adult who’s been savaged by a seal to see the ocean again? All in a day’s job at the South African Marine Rehabilitation and Education Centre where a caregiver’s fingers also narrowly escape the razor-sharp beak of a gannet. Something stands in the way of a high spirited young caracal, but the Sondela Wildlife Centre are hatching a plan to secure his release back in the wild. Two baby warthogs at the Gitta Martula Rehabilitation Wildlife Centre still prefer their apples to their potatoes but, slowly and surely, they are being adjusted to living in their natural environment – the whole wild world awaits.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Rehabilitating and teaching young wild dog packs to hunt is what Margerita does at Erindi Private Game Reserve in Namibia. Margerita had discovered many of the wild dog packs in the region had succumb to illness and died. Margerita took it upon herself to raise an abandoned young pack of wild dogs. She teaches them to run as a pack, hunt as a pack and survive as a pack thus ensuring when the time comes the wild dog pack will survive in the wild. Alma’s passion for yellow baboons in Malawi brought her to the African continent to live-out her passion. Her daily chores, activities and inspections keep her busy. Two yellow baboons meet each other for the first time when the gate separating them is opened. One of South Africa’s smallest antelope known as a Cape Grys Bokkie loves playing hide ‘n seek it seems. When it comes to feeding the young antelope it always proves to be a challenge. However, this could be the nature of this timid little antelope.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: Lindi, the one-eyed Lion cub lives at Wildthingz Lodge situated in the northern region of South Africa. Having one eye does not restrict Lindi from been a playful and energetic lion cub. Skabenga, a young Hyena, also lives at Wildthingz. Skabenga has almost become part of the family, and his bond to his caretaker cannot be denied. Wolves in Africa? Tsitsikamma Wolf Sanctuary is an orphanage to abandoned wolves. Raising wolves on the other hand is not always an easy job. This is the case with an energetic seven-month-old wolf, Raul who knows no boundaries. We meet Victor Hugo at Somkhanda where the motto is to keep all the animals wild while rehabilitating them. We will meet a herd of Zebra and their young and discover that the dynamics in the family group are not always what they seem. Victor also rescues a family of owl hatchlings and ensures their safety by building a new house in a nearby tree.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: It’s not surprising that raising the fastest animal on the planet involves regular exercise; Frank Kasakamula at the Ranch Conservancy in South Africa has a number of strategies in place for the cheetah in his care. A traumatised, orphaned baby Vervet monkey is especially clingy and demanding. But from nappy rash to night feeds, Dean Oakes at the Bambelela Wildlife Sanctuary takes everything involved in being a ‘good mother’ in his stride. Everyone at the Harnas Wildlife Foundation in Namibia has a right to life, including a blind, baby kudu named Derek. Soon his care taker, Louis Duvenhage, will introduce him to its new friends: a Springbok and a Duiker.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: In the Centre for Rehabilitation of Wildlife situated in KwaZulu Natal, South Africa, junior nurse Kylie is preparing grey duiker Major for his release into the wild. Major had suffered several injuries when he arrived at the centre, but with Kylie’s help he recovered, and he will soon be back in his natural environment. We also travel to the Eastern Cape in South Africa to the main port city called, Port Elizabeth. Here Ruth, a dedicated animal caretaker, is constantly monitoring her loggerhead turtle hatchling Rocky, who has trouble with weight fluctuations. But once his nutrition is balanced enough that he can keep his weight for several weeks, he can be released into the Indian Ocean.Deep in the Cameroonian jungles we meet Rachel, a committed caretaker of primates at the Mefoue Primate Sanctuary. She arrived at the centre sixteen years ago and decided to stay after Khan Daniel, a new-born silverback gorilla, chose her as his surrogate mother. Since then her passion for these animals grew stronger from day to day. Sixteen years later Khan Daniel is the leader of the gorilla troop while Rachel now runs the private facility caring for these gentle giants of the forest.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: In this episode, we travel to Howick in Kwa-Zulu Natal, South Africa to a Centre called FreeMe Wildlife Rehabilitation. Here we meet Kirsten, also known as ‘Otter Mom number 1’. Kirsten is training her three orphaned Otters how to swim and hunt. The three mammals, Juliet, Oberon and Othelloe have almost completed their rehabilitation. After preparing the release site for two weeks it is time to return the three siblings back into the river. But persuading them to leave their temporary home might be more of a challenge than Kirsten anticipated. Situated at the base of the Hlumu Hlumu Mountains in the province of Mpumalanga in South Africa we find DIY Wildlife Rehabilitation. Animal caretaker Mitch oversees many different animals at his sanctuary. He is preparing to transport and release a troop of Mongooses onto a new farm. Mongooses play a vital role in the region by keeping the snake population in balance. And in the summer season there are lots of them. So mobilizing the family quickly and efficiently is the first priority. A one-hour drive east of Johannesburg, at Wildlife, Judy is rehabilitating a one month old Squirrel. This tiny rodent has been kept as a pet and was malnourished. Judy constantly has to monitor his weight. But the little animal seems to become stronger and more confident because is obviously doesn’t want to be treated like a baby anymore. So Judy’s efforts pay off in the end.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.sub-title: White Rhino, Giant Pouched Rat, Bat
programme.desc: At the Hoedspruit Endangered Species Centre in the province of Limpopo we meet a member of one of the most threatened species on the planet, a white rhino named Esme. Caretaker Natasha is looking after the nine-month-old and makes sure it can develop in a well-protected surrounding. And she has got some help from a rather unexpected companion for the little calf - a dog named David. Further north we visit Tanzania, where so-called “Hero Rats” are fulfilling special tasks. These giant pouched rats are able to detect landmines and sniff out diseases. One of these little heroes named Sykwalker is learning how to detect tuberculosis. Caretaker Estadiov provides us an insight into the training of these extraordinary animals. In our highlight story, we meet Jane and her orphaned bat Devan. Rewild in Limpopo is the only Centre in the area that cares for and rehabilitates bats. Devan is having trouble with an essential skill for every bat – flying or more specifically the take-off. Because once he is airborne he seems to master the art of flight effortlessly.

programme.title: Raising Wildlife
programme.desc: An hour’s drive from Johannesburg Warren is raising three cheetah cubs at the Lion and Cheetah Sanctuary. They are an endangered species – not only in South Africa but around the world. Warren is taking the three orphaned siblings for walks on a daily basis to give them the opportunity to experience being outside their enclosure. This is part of the preparation for their return to the wild. Convincing them to leave the enclosure is easy, trying to get them back in is a difficult task to undertake. In the South African Limpopo province, we meet clinic manager Nikita at the Moholoholo Animal Rehabilitation Centre. She’s taking care of several different animals all of whom got their own sad story. One of them is a female honey badger who recently lost her cub post-partum. She is mothering a chain as a substitute for her cub so Nikita is trying to help her getting through this difficult phase. She is also monitoring vultures who have fallen victim to poisoning. And she introduces Havana to us - a juvenile caracal who was found abandoned in a hotel. She has obviously been raised by humans as she was found clinging to a milk bottle. In our highlight story we travel to Crag’s View Wild Care Centre in Port Edwards along the south coast of Kwa-Zulu Natal. Here we meet Craig and a partially blind blue duiker. Craig didn’t have high hopes for the antelope to survive, as she has been attacked by a large cat. But thanks to the passionate caretaker the bubbly animal is doing great today.

programme.title: Chiemgau, A Changing Landscape
programme.desc: Three times in the past two decades, the Chiemsee area has experienced "once-in-a-century" floods. Climate, wildlife, and landscape have changed, and it seems this transformation is accelerating. Species living in the flood zones are developing sophisticated survival strategies. Knot ants, for instance, place their queen in the middle and form a raft with their bodies, even conquering new habitats. Flooding is less of a disaster for nature and impacts humans more significantly.

programme.title: Europe's Wilderness
programme.desc: The Bearded Vulture is one of the largest birds of prey in Europe and one of the rarest. With its habitat above the treeline in the alpine mountains, we see one of the largest airworthy birds fly loops during its courtship days and flight for survival on the steep slopes.

programme.title: Destination Wild: India's Lost Worlds
programme.desc: The Western Ghats are amongst some of the most biodiverse regions in the world, and these mountains are the ancestral birthplace for many of India's endemic species. This episode delves into India's jungle heart, and the mysterious forests nestled in the shadow of the Western Ghats' great peaks.

programme.title: Giants of Europe
programme.desc: Once upon a time, huge beasts roamed Europe. But these magnificent creatures were driven out by our greed, and we are only now starting to realise how poor our landscapes are without them. Mercifully, this realisation has started to fuel an exciting change of fortunes for Europe's wildlife.

programme.title: Little Cats
programme.desc: From mysterious lynx to athletic servals and water-bound jungle cats, the little cats of the world are some of the most fascinating and ruthless predators on the planet.

programme.title: Hunters of the Forest
programme.desc: Forests are dark, shady habitats, realms of shadows perfect for predators to lurk. The dense plant growth facilitates a wealth of prey species, and cunning predators of all shapes and sizes have adapted to exploit every niche. Whether crawling on the forest floor, gliding through the trees or stalking the shadows in between, hunters have mastered the terrain in every type of forest imagina-ble. And they're not just stealthy, they're also some of the most enigmatic and beautiful creatures on the planet.

programme.title: Europe's Wilderness
programme.desc: The Grey Seal is Europe's largest water prediator. With its habitat in the fish rich costal areas in cool but not arctic waters we discover that recreation in the sun plays a major role for the seal, but we also see it raiding in dangerous waters.

programme.title: Magical Fjords
programme.desc: Fjords are one of the most dramatic landscapes on Earth. But for all their beauty and majesty, the surface spectacle is just the tip of the iceberg.
